title: Doremy's IQ Test
writeup:
## Reading the problem

We're asked whether we can remove exactly one element from a prefix of the array such that the remaining array, sorted, has a fixed set of consequences on later queries. The key constraint is that queries are monotone in a way that lets us binary search on the answer.

## Key observation

If a candidate index `k` works, every index `k' > k` with the same parity also works. This monotonicity is what unlocks a binary search over the candidate positions instead of a brute-force scan.

## Approach

1. Binary search on the smallest valid index.
2. For each candidate, check feasibility in `O(log n)` using a Fenwick tree over the value range.
3. Maintain the Fenwick tree incrementally as the binary search window moves, so the total complexity stays `O(n log^2 n)`.

```cpp
bool check(int k) {
    // feasibility check using fenwick tree over compressed values
    // returns true if removing prefix[..k] keeps the invariant
    return fenwick.query(lo, hi) == expected;
}
```

## Complexity

`O(n log^2 n)` time, `O(n)` space. Comfortably inside the 2 second limit for `n <= 2*10^5`.

## Takeaway

When a problem's feasibility check is monotone in an index you don't immediately see as "the answer", try binary searching on it directly rather than looking for a closed-form formula.

title: AND Sorting
writeup:
## Idea

We only get to swap pairs `(i, j)` when `a_i AND a_j` equals some fixed mask `k`. The question is what's the largest `k` that still lets us fully sort the array.

## Insight

Any two positions that are *out of order* relative to the sorted array must be swappable, directly or transitively. So `k` must be a submask of `a_i AND a_j` for every misplaced pair `(i, j)`. Taking the AND of all such pairs (equivalently, the AND of `p_i` and `i` for every position where the permutation differs from sorted order) gives the tightest possible `k`.

## Solution

```python
k = (1 << 30) - 1
for i in range(n):
    if a[i] != sorted_a[i]:
        k &= a[i] & sorted_a[i]
print(k)
```

## Why this works

Swapping is transitive when the AND condition holds for a chain of intermediate elements, so we don't need every pair to be directly swappable, just connected through the allowed-swap graph. Restricting `k` to the AND of all *displaced* pairs is both necessary and sufficient.

title: Watermelon
writeup:
## Approach

An even number `w` can be split into two positive even parts unless `w == 2`. Just check `w % 2 == 0 and w > 2`.

```python
w = int(input())
print("YES" if w % 2 == 0 and w > 2 else "NO")
```

A good warm-up problem, nothing more to it than the parity check.
